agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Evolution of the Shipment aggregate from a 1:1 Shipment-Recipient relationship to a 1:N relationship. This is a fundamental domain model change affecting the core bounded context of shipment fulfillment, with downstream impact on 2M existing records and external partner API contracts.","bounded_context":"Shipment Fulfillment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods being transported from an origin to one or more destinations. The root aggregate that owns the delivery lifecycle."},{"term":"Recipient","definition":"A party designated to receive part or all of a Shipment. Identified by name, address, phone number, and now also delivery preferences."},{"term":"Delivery Preferences","definition":"Recipient-specific instructions governing how and when delivery should occur (e.g., time windows, safe-place authorization, signature requirements). New concept introduced with multi-recipient support."},{"term":"Primary Recipient","definition":"The single required recipient on a shipment. Preserves backward compatibility with the prior 1:1 model and serves as the default for legacy integrations."},{"term":"Partner API","definition":"External integration surface that currently consumes shipment data in a single-recipient format. Must be preserved or versioned during migration."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment MUST have at least one Recipient at all times.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A shipment with zero recipients has no delivery destination and is physically undeliverable. The system state would be corrupt.","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to create or update a Shipment that would result in zero Recipients MUST be rejected."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Each Recipient within a Shipment MUST have a unique delivery address.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Two recipients at the same address on the same shipment is either a data error or should be a single recipient with combined items. Allowing duplicates corrupts delivery routing.","testable_assertion":"Adding a Recipient whose address matches an existing Recipient on the same Shipment MUST be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Each Recipient MUST have a name, address, and phone number (the original required fields), plus optional delivery preferences.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"These fields are required for physical delivery and carrier compliance. A recipient without an address cannot receive goods.","testable_assertion":"Creating a Recipient with a null or empty name, address, or phone number MUST be rejected."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A Shipment MUST designate exactly one Primary Recipient when multiple recipients exist.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Operational processes (billing, tracking status, notifications, partner API responses) require a single authoritative recipient as the default. Without this, downstream systems break.","testable_assertion":"A multi-recipient Shipment without exactly one Recipient flagged as primary MUST be rejected."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Legacy single-recipient shipments MUST be migrated to the new model with the existing recipient designated as the Primary Recipient, with no data loss.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a migration policy, not a state integrity constraint. The approach could change (e.g., lazy migration vs. bulk migration).","testable_assertion":"After migration, every pre-existing Shipment has exactly one Recipient marked as primary, and all original name/address/phone data is preserved."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"External partner APIs MUST continue to receive shipment data in the original single-recipient format unless the partner has opted into the multi-recipient schema.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a backward-compatibility policy that can be relaxed per partner as they upgrade.","testable_assertion":"Partner API responses for non-opted-in partners MUST return the Primary Recipient's data in the original flat format with no breaking field changes."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"Delivery preferences are scoped per Recipient, not per Shipment.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"The entire business motivation for multi-recipient is that each recipient can have different delivery preferences. If preferences are shipment-level, the model change is pointless.","testable_assertion":"Two Recipients on the same Shipment can have different delivery preference values, and each is honored independently during delivery."},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"Splitting a Shipment across multiple Recipients does not change the total item count — items must be allocated to Recipients such that every item is assigned to exactly one Recipient.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Items appearing on zero or multiple recipients means physical goods are lost or duplicated. This is an accounting and fulfillment integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"The sum of items across all Recipients in a Shipment MUST equal the total Shipment item count, with no item assigned to more than one Recipient."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A new Shipment is being created","when":"The creator specifies one Recipient with name, address, phone, and delivery preferences","then":"The Shipment is created with that Recipient automatically designated as Primary Recipient"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A Shipment with one Recipient exists","when":"A second Recipient is added with a different address","then":"The Shipment now has two Recipients, the original remains Primary, and both have independently configurable delivery preferences"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A Shipment with two Recipients exists","when":"A user attempts to remove the last remaining Recipient","then":"The operation is rejected with a validation error stating at least one Recipient is required"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A Shipment with multiple Recipients exists","when":"The Primary Recipient is removed","then":"The system requires a new Primary Recipient to be designated before the removal is committed"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A legacy Shipment (pre-migration) with one Recipient exists in production","when":"The data migration runs","then":"The Recipient is preserved with all original fields intact and flagged as Primary Recipient"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment exists","when":"An external partner API that has NOT opted into multi-recipient queries the shipment","then":"The API returns the Primary Recipient's data in the original single-recipient flat format"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment exists","when":"An external partner API that HAS opted into multi-recipient queries the shipment","then":"The API returns all Recipients with their individual delivery preferences in the new schema"},{"id":"AC-8","given":"A Shipment with Recipient A at address X exists","when":"A user attempts to add Recipient B at the same address X","then":"The operation is rejected with a duplicate-address validation error"},{"id":"AC-9","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment with items","when":"Items are allocated to Recipients","then":"Every item is assigned to exactly one Recipient, and the total matches the Shipment item count"},{"id":"AC-10","given":"A Shipment with multiple Recipients each having different delivery preferences","when":"The delivery process executes","then":"Each Recipient's delivery is fulfilled according to their individual preferences, not the Shipment-level default"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Partner API receives a shipment update where the Primary Recipient changes","expected_behavior":"The partner API (non-opted-in) now returns the new Primary Recipient's data. Partners must be notified that the 'recipient' they see may change identity. Consider whether this breaks partner assumptions."},{"scenario":"A Recipient's address is updated to match another Recipient's address on the same Shipment","expected_behavior":"The update is rejected with a duplicate-address validation error, same as creation."},{"scenario":"All Recipients except one are removed, and that remaining one was not the Primary","expected_behavior":"The last remaining Recipient is automatically promoted to Primary Recipient."},{"scenario":"A Shipment is created with multiple Recipients but no items have been allocated yet","expected_behavior":"The Shipment is valid but in a 'pending allocation' state. Item allocation must occur before the Shipment can transition to fulfillment."},{"scenario":"Bulk migration of 2M records fails partway through","expected_behavior":"Migration must be idempotent and resumable. Already-migrated records are not re-processed. Failed records are logged for retry. No shipment is left in an inconsistent half-migrated state."},{"scenario":"A partner API call arrives during the migration window for a not-yet-migrated shipment","expected_behavior":"The system serves the shipment in whatever format it currently exists in — old format for unmigrated, new format (with primary recipient flattened) for migrated. No errors due to migration state."},{"scenario":"Recipient is added with delivery preferences but the carrier for that route does not support those preferences","expected_behavior":"The system should validate delivery preferences against carrier capabilities at assignment time and warn or reject unsupported preferences."},{"scenario":"A Shipment has 50+ Recipients","expected_behavior":"System should enforce a reasonable upper bound on recipients per shipment to prevent abuse and operational complexity. The exact limit is a policy decision."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Multi-recipient shipments can consolidate what were previously separate shipments into one, reducing shipping costs. However, failed migration or partner API breakage could cause revenue loss from integration failures. The 2M record migration has a cost in engineering time and potential downtime.","operational":"Significant increase in fulfillment complexity — dispatchers and warehouse staff now handle item allocation across recipients. Routing algorithms must account for multiple drop-off points. Support teams need training on the new model. Migration of 2M records requires a carefully planned rollout.","user":"Customers gain the ability to send a single shipment to multiple people (e.g., gift orders, business distribution). This is a meaningful UX improvement. However, the multi-recipient interface must remain simple for the common single-recipient case — the 1:1 path should not become more complex.","risk":"HIGH — This is a core domain model change. Risks include: (1) Data migration failure corrupting 2M production records, (2) External partner API breakage causing integration outages, (3) Inconsistent state during migration window, (4) Item allocation bugs causing lost or duplicated goods, (5) Performance degradation from 1:N joins on a high-volume table.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End Customers","impact":"Gain multi-recipient capability. Single-recipient experience must remain unchanged in complexity."},{"group":"Operations / Dispatchers","impact":"Must learn new item allocation workflow. Routing becomes more complex with multi-stop deliveries."},{"group":"External Partners","impact":"API contract changes. Non-opted-in partners must see zero breaking changes. Opted-in partners need documentation and testing time."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Schema migration, API versioning, backward compatibility layer, performance testing for 1:N relationships at scale."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"New failure modes to troubleshoot — partial deliveries, per-recipient preference issues, primary recipient disputes."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Billing model may need revision — is a multi-recipient shipment billed as one shipment or per-recipient? This is unspecified."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The core model evolution is well-defined (1:1 to 1:N), and the migration constraint is concrete (2M records, partner APIs). However, several critical business details are unspecified: how items are allocated across recipients, billing model for multi-recipient shipments, delivery preference taxonomy, upper bounds on recipients, and the exact partner opt-in mechanism. These are not contradictions — they are aspects the product team has not yet specified.","high_confidence_areas":["The Recipient must remain a required entity with at least one per Shipment","Primary Recipient designation is necessary for backward compatibility","Partner API backward compatibility is non-negotiable for non-opted-in partners","Migration must be lossless and preserve existing recipient data","Delivery preferences are per-recipient, not per-shipment"],"low_confidence_areas":["Billing model for multi-recipient shipments — is it per-shipment or per-recipient?","Maximum number of recipients allowed per shipment","Exact taxonomy of delivery preferences — what options exist?","Whether multi-recipient means multi-destination (split delivery) or single-destination with multiple receivers","Partner opt-in mechanism and timeline","Item allocation rules when items are not explicitly assigned"],"assumptions":["Multi-recipient implies multiple delivery destinations, not multiple people at one address","A Primary Recipient concept is needed even though the product spec did not explicitly state it","Items must be allocated to individual recipients rather than delivered in full to each","The partner API contract is REST/JSON-based with a flat recipient structure"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec says 'one or more Recipients' but does not clarify whether this means multi-destination delivery (split shipment across locations) or multi-party delivery (multiple named receivers, potentially at the same address). These are fundamentally different fulfillment models with different routing, billing, and tracking implications.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Engineering","End Customers"],"options":["Multi-destination: each Recipient has a unique address, shipment is physically split across locations","Multi-party: multiple named receivers can share an address, single physical delivery with multiple notifications","Hybrid: allow both, with business rules governing each case"],"recommendation":"Clarify with product team. Recommend multi-destination as the primary model (unique address per recipient enforced), since this is the higher-value logistics capability. Multi-party at same address can be modeled as a single Recipient with multiple contact persons."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Billing model for multi-recipient shipments is unspecified. A shipment going to 3 addresses has radically different cost structure than one going to 1 address. Is this billed as one shipment, or per-recipient, or by total distance?","affected_stakeholders":["Finance / Billing","End Customers","External Partners"],"options":["Per-shipment flat rate regardless of recipient count","Per-recipient pricing (each recipient = separate delivery fee)","Distance/weight-based pricing that accounts for multi-stop routing"],"recommendation":"Per-recipient pricing is most aligned with physical delivery costs and industry norms. Each recipient incurs routing, handling, and last-mile cost. Recommend per-recipient billing with a bundled discount."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Delivery preferences' is introduced as a new concept but the spec provides no taxonomy of what preferences exist. Without this, the domain model cannot be properly bounded.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Operations","End Customers"],"options":["Define a fixed enum of preferences (time window, safe place, signature required, contactless)","Allow freeform key-value preferences","Carrier-dependent preference sets"],"recommendation":"Define a core set of structured preferences (time window, signature requirement, safe-place authorization, delivery instructions) as a value object. Allow carrier-specific extensions but enforce a base schema."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

content.thinking: Let me analyze this domain model evolution from single-recipient to multi-recipient shipments.
